title: The West can learn from Ukraine’s AI future vision beyond the battlefield
gist: Ukraine has become a live testing ground not only for AI-enabled weaponry but for an 'agentic state' of citizen-facing government AI agents, launching Diia.AI (built on Google Gemini Flash 2.0) as the first national-government AI agent and moving the Ministry of Defense to AI-driven drone procurement to cut corruption — a model the article argues the West should study and adapt.

insights.claim: Ukraine launched Diia.AI in fall 2025, built by Ukrainian IT company Kitsoft on Google's Gemini Flash 2.0, billed as the first AI agent for a national government to deliver bureaucratic services (e.g., tax documents) directly to citizens.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: First-mover national-scale gov AI agent built on Google's model is a reference deployment and a template Western governments may license/replicate, a potential demand channel for foundation-model vendors in public sector.
insights.quote: Built by Ukrainian IT company Kitsoft using Google’s Gemini Flash 2.0 model, Diia.AI is the first AI agent for a national government to provide simple bureaucratic services to citizens.
insights.locator: Ukraine is also moving quickly to deploy

insights.claim: Ukraine's Acting Minister of Digital Transformation Oleksandr Bornyakov (who replaced Fedorov in January) is building an 'agentic state' — an ecosystem of AI agents to carry out a range of bureaucratic functions.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Signals a sustained government program (not a one-off pilot) creating recurring public-sector demand for agentic AI infrastructure and integration vendors.
insights.quote: Bornyakov has since forged ahead with plans to build the “agentic state,” an ecosystem of AI agents to carry out a range of bureaucratic functions.
insights.locator: Efforts are underway to broaden the array

insights.claim: The article proposes a low-risk adoption pattern for Western governments: train a top-quality model on existing government data for a single narrow use case (e.g., filing an individual tax document) with security guardrails, then iterate — analogous to procuring micro-quantities of drones for battlefield testing before bulk buying.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: A 'narrow-pilot-then-scale' playbook implies fragmented, per-use-case gov AI procurement rather than monolithic contracts — favoring nimble integrators over big-bang platform plays.
insights.quote: Piloting an agent for one particular use minimizes risk and makes it easier to iterate and fine tune, much as governments procure micro quantities of drones for battlefield testing before buying in bulk.
insights.locator: It might be possible for Western governments

insights.claim: Governments that fail to integrate AI-enabled services risk growing friction with increasingly AI-native populations and could miss opportunities to shape new technology development.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Frames gov AI adoption as a competitive/legitimacy imperative, supporting a secular tailwind for govtech and public-sector AI spend.
insights.quote: governments that do not integrate AI-enabled services risk growing friction with increasingly AI-native populations
insights.locator: Nevertheless, approaching agentic AI as a means

insights.claim: Ukraine's Ministry of Defense, now led by former digital minister Mykhailo Fedorov, has stated ambitions to deploy fully autonomous front-line defenses and is moving forward with partially autonomous systems to offset a manpower disadvantage against Russia.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Ukraine's willingness to field autonomous weapons faster than Western norms allow makes it the proving ground (and partner/customer) for autonomy-focused defense-tech firms.
insights.quote: Ukraine has different priorities and is moving forward with partially autonomous systems to combat a significant manpower disadvantage against an imminent threat to its existence.
insights.locator: It is clear that innovation has won out

insights.claim: Debates over autonomous weapons systems in the US have caused major fissures between the US government and AI companies, a constraint Ukraine does not share.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: US ethical/political friction creates a regulatory-arbitrage gap that pushes autonomy R&D and validation toward Ukraine, shaping where defense-AI capability accrues.
insights.quote: In the United States, debates about autonomous weapons systems have led to major fissures between the US government and AI companies.
insights.locator: It is clear that innovation has won out

insights.claim: Ukraine's Ministry of Defense will now rely on AI to process battlefield data and decide which drones to procure, explicitly aiming to minimize the corruption risks of human-centric procurement.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: AI-driven, performance-based procurement reshapes who wins drone contracts (data-validated effectiveness over relationships) and is a governance/anti-corruption use case exportable to other defense ministries.
insights.quote: the Ministry of Defense will now rely on AI to process battlefield data and determine which drones it should procure, aiming to maximize effectiveness and minimize the corruption risks
insights.locator: The pressure on Kyiv to simultaneously defend

insights.claim: Ukraine's Brave1 defense accelerator supplies fledgling defense startups with grants, data, and software to develop and scale products at speed, drawing European and American officials and defense leaders to partner with Ukrainian military-tech firms.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Brave1 is a deal-flow funnel into battle-tested defense startups; Western primes and investors seeking validated drone/counter-UAV tech will route through it.
insights.quote: the country’s Brave1 defense accelerator provides fledgling defense startups with grants, data, and software to develop and scale their products at speed.
insights.locator: Fedorov is now Ukraine’s Minister of Defense

themes.theme: Drivers and risks of Ukraine's AI push

insights.claim: Ukraine is leaning into AI for both weaponry and government services because of a human-capital gap — its most talented people are absorbed by defense — making automation essential to maintain state function and defense simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Labor-scarcity-driven adoption is the structural reason Ukraine moves faster than peers; demographic/manpower pressure is the underlying signal for where aggressive automation emerges.
insights.quote: Ukraine is leaning into AI for government services for the same reason it has become a world leader in AI-enabled weaponry: The country faces a human capital gap.
insights.locator: Ukraine is leaning into AI for government services

insights.claim: Former deputy minister Valeriya Ionan frames agentic systems as the way to 'manage complexity and decision-making at a scale no workforce can match,' positioning AI as a substitute for unavailable headcount rather than a mere efficiency tool.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: The 'scale beyond any workforce' framing is the bull case for agentic AI in any labor-constrained institution, public or private.
insights.quote: Agentic systems expand the ability to manage complexity and decision-making at a scale no workforce can match.
insights.locator: Ukraine is leaning into AI for government services

insights.claim: Rapid roll-out of consumer-facing public and private AI products during Ukraine's recovery is expected to intensify the innovation-versus-security debate and raise data-privacy and platform-security concerns among the public and experts.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Privacy/security backlash is the foreseeable friction point that could slow or reshape gov AI deployments — a risk to watch for govtech adoption timelines.
insights.quote: concerns about data privacy and platform security will also surely crop up, both among the Ukrainian public and in the expert community.
insights.locator: The innovation versus security debate will certainly

insights.claim: Ukraine's regulatory strategy (per then-minister Fedorov in 2023) is to first let AI innovation flourish under mostly voluntary oversight, then later draft legislation to harmonize with EU rules as accession negotiations proceed.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: A deliberate 'innovate-first, regulate-later, harmonize-with-EU' sequence creates a temporary permissive window — relevant for where AI products get piloted before EU AI Act-style constraints bite.
insights.quote: an approach aimed at initially allowing AI innovation to flourish with mostly voluntary government oversight, before later drafting legislation to harmonize Ukraine’s regulations with those of the European Union

insights.claim: AI-enabled citizen services could entice Ukrainian professionals who left after the 2022 invasion to return by guaranteeing seamless business registration and transparency, and help displaced people recover normalcy via smartphone-based services.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Frames govtech as a talent-retention and reconstruction lever — a second-order economic argument tying digital state quality to human-capital repatriation.
insights.quote: Ukrainian professionals who moved abroad after the 2022 invasion might be more easily enticed to return home if the state can better guarantee seamless business registration and transparency.
insights.locator: Bornyakov and the Ministry of Digital Transformation
